questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem:
Passage: The Industrial Revolution, which began in Britain in the late 18th century, was powered largely by coal. Steam engines, fuelled by coal, drove factories, railways, and ships, transforming economies and societies. The demand for coal led to the expansion of mining operations across Europe and America. However, the social cost was high, with poor working conditions, child labour, and environmental pollution. The legacy of the Industrial Revolution continues to influence modern industrial practices and environmental policies. Today, many former mining regions are being revitalised through diversification and clean energy initiatives.

The word 'legacy' in the passage most nearly means:

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Meaning of 'To face the music':

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem:
Passage: The Gondwana coal fields of eastern India contain the country's best quality coal. These fields stretch across Jharkhand, West Bengal, Odisha, and Chhattisgarh. The coal found here is primarily bituminous, which has high calorific value and low ash content. In contrast, the Tertiary coal fields of northeastern India contain lignite and sub-bituminous coal. Coal grading is based on ash content, with higher grades having lower ash percentages. Washeries are used to reduce ash content and improve coal quality before it is supplied to power plants and industries.

Tertiary coal fields of northeastern India contain:

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Meaning of 'A piece of cake':

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem:
Passage: Underground mining is one of the most hazardous occupations in the world. The primary risks include roof falls, gas explosions, flooding, and ventilation failures. In India, the Mines Act of 1952 and the Coal Mines Regulations of 2017 govern safety standards. Modern technology has significantly improved mine safety through real-time monitoring systems, gas detectors, and automated equipment. Despite these advances, safety remains a critical concern, and regular training programs are mandatory for all mine workers. The introduction of continuous miners and longwall mining methods has increased both productivity and safety.

The Coal Mines Regulations were enacted in:

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Meaning of 'To spill the beans':

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem:
Passage: The Gondwana coal fields of eastern India contain the country's best quality coal. These fields stretch across Jharkhand, West Bengal, Odisha, and Chhattisgarh. The coal found here is primarily bituminous, which has high calorific value and low ash content. In contrast, the Tertiary coal fields of northeastern India contain lignite and sub-bituminous coal. Coal grading is based on ash content, with higher grades having lower ash percentages. Washeries are used to reduce ash content and improve coal quality before it is supplied to power plants and industries.

Gondwana coal fields are located in which part of India?

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Meaning of 'To give the benefit of the doubt':

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem:
Passage: Coal is the most abundant fossil fuel in India and plays a crucial role in the country's energy mix. India has the fourth-largest coal reserves in the world, with an estimated 344 billion tonnes. Coal India Limited (CIL) is the largest coal-producing company globally, accounting for about 82% of India's coal output. However, concerns about environmental degradation and climate change have prompted a shift towards cleaner energy sources like solar and wind power. The government has set a target of achieving 500 GW of renewable energy capacity by 2030.

Which of the following is mentioned as a concern related to coal usage?

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Meaning of 'To throw in the towel':

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem:
Passage: India's energy demand is projected to grow at 3-4% annually over the next decade. While coal remains the dominant source, accounting for about 55% of installed capacity, renewable energy is expanding rapidly. Solar power capacity has grown from just 2.6 GW in 2014 to over 70 GW in 2024. Wind power contributes another 45 GW. The government's Production-Linked Incentive (PLI) scheme for solar manufacturing aims to reduce dependence on imports. Nuclear power, with 7.5 GW capacity, and hydropower, with 47 GW, are other important components of India's energy portfolio.

India's solar power capacity in 2024 is approximately:

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Meaning of 'To break the ice':

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem:
Passage: The Gondwana coal fields of eastern India contain the country's best quality coal. These fields stretch across Jharkhand, West Bengal, Odisha, and Chhattisgarh. The coal found here is primarily bituminous, which has high calorific value and low ash content. In contrast, the Tertiary coal fields of northeastern India contain lignite and sub-bituminous coal. Coal grading is based on ash content, with higher grades having lower ash percentages. Washeries are used to reduce ash content and improve coal quality before it is supplied to power plants and industries.

The coal found in Gondwana fields is primarily:

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Meaning of 'To have a chip on one's shoulder':

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem:
Passage: The Industrial Revolution, which began in Britain in the late 18th century, was powered largely by coal. Steam engines, fuelled by coal, drove factories, railways, and ships, transforming economies and societies. The demand for coal led to the expansion of mining operations across Europe and America. However, the social cost was high, with poor working conditions, child labour, and environmental pollution. The legacy of the Industrial Revolution continues to influence modern industrial practices and environmental policies. Today, many former mining regions are being revitalised through diversification and clean energy initiatives.

Which of the following was a social cost of the Industrial Revolution?
options: Poor working conditions and child labour
questions.solution: Poor working conditions and child labour

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Meaning of 'To grease the wheels':

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem:
Passage: Coal is the most abundant fossil fuel in India and plays a crucial role in the country's energy mix. India has the fourth-largest coal reserves in the world, with an estimated 344 billion tonnes. Coal India Limited (CIL) is the largest coal-producing company globally, accounting for about 82% of India's coal output. However, concerns about environmental degradation and climate change have prompted a shift towards cleaner energy sources like solar and wind power. The government has set a target of achieving 500 GW of renewable energy capacity by 2030.

According to the passage, India has the ____ largest coal reserves in the world.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: She is proud ____ her achievements.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Find the error: The team (A) / are working (B) / on the new (C) / project since May. (D)

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: The success of the mission ____ on teamwork.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Find the error: She is (A) / one of the (B) / most talented (C) / singer in the industry. (D)

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Antonym of CONCISE:

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Find the error: She is (A) / more taller (B) / than her (C) / younger sister. (D)

questions.stem: Antonym of DISPARITY:

questions.stem: Antonym of PERSEVERANCE:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A worker's observed time is 4 min at a performance rating of 120%. The normal time is:

questions.subject: Industrial Engineering
questions.stem: Branch and bound: bounding function provides:
questions.solution: B&B: prunes branches when bound worse than current best. Upper bound (maximization) or lower bound (minimization).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Joint replenishment — can-order policy:
options: Coordinated replenishment for multiple items from same supplier. Major setup + minor setup per item
questions.solution: Joint: (s,c,S) policy. c = can-order point. When any item hits s, check others below c. Aggregate savings.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: An assembly line has total task time 40 min, 6 stations and a cycle time of 12 min. Its balance efficiency is:

questions.subject: Industrial Engineering
questions.stem: MoSCoW prioritization: MoSCoW stands for:
questions.solution: MoSCoW: Must (critical), Should (important), Could (desirable), Won't (not this time). Helps manage scope.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Warehouse — slotting optimization:
options: Place fast-movers in golden zone. Minimize travel distance. Cube utilization. Cobra effect
questions.solution: Slotting: Pareto on picks. Family grouping (similar items together). Forward pick area replenishment.

questions.subject: Industrial Engineering
questions.stem: Economies of scale: average cost decreases because:
options: Fixed costs spread over more units
questions.solution: Scale: spreading fixed cost over more units. Also: bulk purchasing, specialization, better technology. Limits: diseconomies of scale.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Customers arrive at 15/hour and spend on average 0.5 hour in the system. The average number in the system is:

questions.subject: Industrial Engineering
questions.stem: Warehouse management system (WMS) optimizes:
questions.solution: WMS: real-time inventory tracking, optimized operations.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: EWMA (Exponentially Weighted Moving Average):
options: z_t = λ·x̄_t + (1-λ)z_{t-1}. λ = 0.2 typical. Weighted average of all past data. Detects small shifts
questions.solution: EWMA: λ small → more weight on past. Control limits: UCL = μ_0 + L·σ·√(λ/(2-λ)(1-(1-λ)^{2t})). Stable limits.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: U-shaped manufacturing cell:
options: Workers can tend multiple machines. Shorter walking distance. Communication easier
questions.solution: U-cell: lean manufacturing. Cross-trained workers. In-and-out flow. Flexibility.

questions.subject: Industrial Engineering
questions.stem: Quality Cost (COQ) categories include:
options: Prevention, Appraisal, Internal failure, External failure
questions.solution: COQ: four categories. Prevention (training, design) + Appraisal (inspection) + Internal failure (scrap) + External failure (warranty).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: An activity's normal time/cost are 8 days/₹4000 and crash time/cost are 4 days/₹8000. Its crash cost slope is:
questions.solution: Cost slope = (crash − normal cost)/(normal − crash time) = (8000−4000)/(8−4) = ₹1000/day.

questions.subject: Industrial Engineering
questions.stem: RULA (Rapid Upper Limb Assessment) score of 5-6 indicates:
questions.solution: RULA: 1-2 acceptable, 3-4 investigate, 5-6 change soon, 7+ change immediately. Upper limb disorder risk.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Neural network forecasting (NNAR):
options: NNAR(p,k): p = lags, k = hidden. One-step ahead prediction. Non-linear. No interpretable parameters
questions.solution: NNAR: feedforward. ReLu/sigmoid activation. Ensemble (multiple random starts). Not extrapolate trend well.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: An assembly line has total task time 54 min, 6 stations and a cycle time of 10 min. Its balance efficiency is:

questions.subject: Industrial Engineering
questions.stem: Resource leveling in project scheduling:
questions.solution: Leveling: extend schedule to resolve resource conflicts. May increase project duration. Uses float.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Fixed cost = ₹20000, price = ₹80/unit, variable cost = ₹30/unit. The break-even quantity is:

questions.subject: Industrial Engineering
questions.stem: Break-even analysis: contribution margin equals:
options: Selling price per unit minus variable cost per unit
questions.solution: Contribution margin: revenue per unit available to cover fixed costs and profit. Break-even: Q = FC/CM per unit.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Condition monitoring techniques:
options: Vibration analysis, oil analysis, thermography, ultrasonic, motor current analysis
questions.solution: Vibration: FFT spectrum. Oil: wear debris. Thermography: hot spots. Ultrasonic: leak detection.

questions.subject: Industrial Engineering
questions.stem: Input-Output Control: monitors:
options: Work released vs work completed at work centers
questions.solution: I/O control: tracks planned input vs actual input, planned output vs actual output. Identifies bottlenecks.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The last three demands were 100, 150 and 140 units. The 3-period moving-average forecast is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: AGV — navigation vs control:
options: Dead reckoning (wire, magnetic tape), Laser (reflectors), Vision (SLAM). Centralized vs distributed control
questions.solution: AGV: LiDAR-based SLAM for autonomous. Fleet management: traffic avoidance, battery charging.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: In acceptance sampling, a Type I error (producer's risk) means:
questions.solution: Type I (α): a genuinely acceptable lot is rejected; Type II (β): a bad lot is accepted.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: VE — function worth determination:
options: Worth = least cost to perform function. Value = worth/cost. V < 1 = VE opportunity. Target cost from worth
questions.solution: Function worth: compare to external benchmarks. Reference: competitive products. Ve ratio improvement.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: VE — life cycle cost components:
options: R&D cost, production cost (materials, labor, overhead), operating cost, disposal cost
questions.solution: LCC: 70-80% in early design. VE targets downstream costs. Cost commitment curve.

questions.subject: Industrial Engineering
questions.stem: Cp = 1.0 means:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The break-even point is the output level at which:
questions.solution: At break-even, contribution covers fixed cost exactly, so profit is zero.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Cross-docking: types include pre-distribution and post-distribution:
options: Pre: labeled at source. Post: sorted at DC. Both reduce storage time
questions.solution: Pre-distribution: already sorted at origin. Post-distribution: sort at cross-dock. Reduces handling.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: MOST (Maynard):
options: Sequence models (A, B, C activity sequences). Faster than MTM-1
questions.solution: MOST: 3 sequence models. General move, controlled move, tool use. Time in TMU.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Naive forecast: F_{t+1} = D_t. For seasonal data:
options: Seasonal naive: F_{t+1} = D_{t-s+1} (same period last season)
questions.solution: Naive: last observation. Seasonal naive: same season last year. Simple but often hard to beat.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Linear Programming (LP):
options: Maximize/minimize objective function subject to constraints. Simplex method
questions.solution: LP: decision variables, objective function, constraints (≤, =, ≥). Feasible region = convex polygon.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Daily demand = 40, lead time = 5 days, safety stock = 80 units. The reorder point is:
questions.solution: ROP = (demand × lead time) + safety stock = 40×5 + 80 = 280 units.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Which comparison of system reliability is correct?
options: A parallel arrangement is more reliable than the same components in series
Series is more reliable than parallel
questions.solution: Series reliability is the product (lower than any part); parallel redundancy raises reliability.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Value analysis vs value engineering:
options: VA: on existing product. VE: during design phase (more effective)
questions.solution: VE: during design (more freedom). VA: on existing (less freedom). Both use function analysis.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: TRIZ (Theory of Inventive Problem Solving):
options: 40 inventive principles. Contradiction matrix. Technical and physical contradictions
questions.solution: TRIZ: Altshuller. System of engineering parameters. Separation principles for physical contradictions.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Ve types of value:
options: Use value, Esteem value, Exchange value, Cost value
questions.solution: Use: functional. Esteem: prestige. Exchange: resale. VE improves use value per unit cost.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: VE job plan: information phase:
options: Gather data on current product/function/cost. Functional analysis.
questions.solution: VE: 7 phases. Information: understand current state. Function: what does it do? Cost: how much?

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Daily demand = 60, lead time = 6 days, safety stock = 50 units. The reorder point is:
questions.solution: ROP = (demand × lead time) + safety stock = 60×6 + 50 = 410 units.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Average Outgoing Quality (AOQ):
options: Expected quality after inspection. AOQL = maximum AOQ
questions.solution: AOQ = P_a·p·(N-n)/N (with rectification). AOQL: worst-case AOQ.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Two-handed process chart:
options: Simultaneous movements of both hands. Symbology same as flow chart
questions.solution: Two-handed: for repetitive assembly tasks. Improve motion economy.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Fixed cost = ₹20000, price = ₹100/unit, variable cost = ₹60/unit. The break-even quantity is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: On the control chart shown (CL = 60, UCL = 69, LCL = 51), the highlighted point lying above the UCL indicates that the process is:
options: In control with only chance variation
Out of control (an assignable cause is present)
questions.solution: A point beyond the ±3σ control limits (UCL = 69) signals an assignable cause — the process is out of statistical control.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: TPM — 8 pillars:
options: Autonomous maintenance, Planned, Quality, Focused improvement, Training, Safety, TPM office, Environment
questions.solution: TPM: Nakajima. Autonomous: operator cleans, inspects, lubricates. Skills: basic, improvement, diagnostic.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Material handling principles: Unit load principle:
options: Move materials in largest practical units to minimize handling次数
questions.solution: Unit load: maximize load size. Pallets, containers. Reduces handling frequency and cost.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The last three demands were 120, 110 and 140 units. The 3-period moving-average forecast is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Multi-echelon inventory: echelon stock = inventory on hand + pipeline stock (in-transit):
options: Total stock from current to downstream
questions.solution: Echelon: total stock position. Includes downstream inventory. Important for coordination.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Daily demand = 60, lead time = 6 days, safety stock = 80 units. The reorder point is:
questions.solution: ROP = (demand × lead time) + safety stock = 60×6 + 80 = 440 units.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Activities on the critical path have a total float of:
questions.solution: Any delay on a zero-float (critical) activity delays the whole project.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Scheduling — forward vs backward:
options: Forward: from start (earliest). Backward: from due date (latest)
questions.solution: Forward: minimizes idle time. Backward: minimizes WIP. Lead time offset.

questions.subject: Industrial Engineering
questions.stem: Simplex tableau: most negative indicator in Z-row indicates:
options: Variable that improves objective most per unit increase
questions.solution: Z-row: reduced costs. Most negative = steepest improvement.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Performance rating:
options: Worker speed/pace relative to normal (100%). Westinghouse system: skill, effort, conditions, consistency
questions.solution: Rating: 100 = normal pace (walking 5 km/h). Below 100 = slow, above = fast.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Fixed cost = ₹10000, price = ₹80/unit, variable cost = ₹30/unit. The break-even quantity is:

questions.subject: Industrial Engineering
questions.stem: MAD measures:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: MRP explosion: gross requirements = independent demand + dependent demand from:
options: Parent item's planned order releases × quantity per parent
questions.solution: MRP: dependent demand from BOM. Gross req → net req (after inventory) → planned orders.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A simple moving-average forecast gives:
options: Weight only to the latest period
Equal weight to each period in the window
questions.solution: An n-period moving average averages the last n observations equally.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Firmed planned orders (FPOs):
options: Planned orders that are frozen by planner to prevent MRP system from changing them
questions.solution: FPO: override MRP. Frozen in near term. Manual control of scheduling.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Delphi method steps:
options: 1) Select experts, 2) Round 1 questionnaire, 3) Anonymize and summarize, 4) Round 2 (repeat until convergence)
questions.solution: Delphi: iterative anonymous surveys. Facilitator summarizes between rounds. Avoids groupthink.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Bottleneck management (TOC):
questions.solution: TOC: throughput, inventory, operating expense. Drum-Buffer-Rope (DBR). Theory of Constraints.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: With α = 0.3, last actual = 200 and last forecast = 220, the next exponential-smoothing forecast is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Batch splitting (splitting lots):
options: Divide batch into sub-batches for parallel processing. Reduce throughput time
questions.solution: Split: 2+ machines simultaneously. Overlapping: start next operation before batch complete.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Forecasting — drift method:
options: Naive + average historical change. F_{t+h} = A_t + h·(A_t-A_1)/(t-1)
questions.solution: Drift: captures trend. Average change per period. Between naive and linear regression.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Routing:
options: Determines path and sequence of operations (machine, process sequence)
questions.solution: Route sheet: operation sequence, machine, tools, time. Bill of materials.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Which statement about float (slack) is correct?
options: All activities have the same float
Critical activities have zero total float
questions.solution: Zero float identifies critical activities; any delay there delays the project.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Decision — utility theory:
questions.solution: Utility: U(x) represents preference under uncertainty. Certainty equivalent < expected value for risk-averse.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Supply chain metrics: perfect order rate:
options: % of orders delivered on time, complete, damage-free, with correct documentation
questions.solution: Perfect order: all conditions met. Industry target: >95%. Measures customer experience.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For the CPM network shown (path 1-2-4 = A+B = 5+6, path 1-3-4 = C+D = 3+5), the project duration (critical path) is:
questions.solution: Path durations: 1-2-4 = 11, 1-3-4 = 8. The critical path is the longest = 11 days.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Associative (causal) forecasting:
options: Uses explanatory variables (e.g., advertising → sales). Regression model
questions.solution: Associative: y = f(x₁, x₂, ...). Multiple regression. Correlation ≠ causation.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Work Breakdown Structure (WBS): decomposition of project into:
options: Deliverables (products, services) at increasing levels of detail
questions.solution: WBS: deliverable-oriented decomposition. 100% rule: sum of children = parent. No overlap.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Method study (motion study) is primarily concerned with:
options: Finding the best way to do a job
Finding the time a job should take
questions.solution: Method study analyses and improves work methods; time study establishes the standard time.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: OEE calculation:
options: OEE = Availability × Performance × Quality. Availability = (Loading - Downtime)/Loading
questions.solution: OEE: Loading: planned production time. Performance: (ideal cycle × output)/operating time. Quality: good/total.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Kanban system:
options: Card signal for production/pull. Number of kanbans = (D·L·(1+α))/C
questions.solution: Kanban: withdrawal and production kanban. α = safety factor. C = container capacity.

questions.subject: Industrial Engineering
questions.stem: Transportation problem degeneracy occurs when:
options: Number of basic variables < m + n - 1
questions.solution: Degeneracy: fewer than m+n-1 basic cells.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Stakeholder management — salience model:
options: Power, legitimacy, urgency → dormant, demanding, dominant, dangerous, dependent, definitive
questions.solution: Salience: definitive (all 3). Latent: power only. Expectant: 2 of 3. Mitchell, Agle, Wood (1997).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Game theory — pure vs mixed strategy:
options: Pure: always choose same action. Mixed: randomized choice (probability distribution)
questions.solution: Mixed: when no saddle point. Solve by linear programming or formula for 2×2.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For D = 3600/yr, S = ₹50, H = ₹2/unit/yr, the minimum total ordering-plus-holding cost (at EOQ ≈ 424.3) is about:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A product is used at 80 units/day with a lead time of 3 days. The reorder point (no safety stock) is:
questions.solution: ROP = demand rate × lead time = 80×3 = 240 units.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Lot-for-lot ordering:
options: Order exactly what is needed each period. No inventory. Used in MRP/JIT
questions.solution: Lot-for-lot: zero holding, high order cost. For expensive or perishable items.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A product is used at 30 units/day with a lead time of 5 days. The reorder point (no safety stock) is:
questions.solution: ROP = demand rate × lead time = 30×5 = 150 units.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Pareto chart:
options: Bar chart ranked by frequency + cumulative percentage line
questions.solution: Pareto: 80/20 rule. Bars: decreasing frequency. Line: cumulative %. Focus on vital few.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Six Sigma: DPMO = (Defects / Opportunities) × 10⁶. For 3 defects in 1000 units with 5 opportunities:
questions.solution: DPMO = D/(U×O) × 10⁶. D=3, U=1000, O=5. DPMO = 600. Sigma level ≈ 4.6.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: X-bar chart: X̄=100, R̄=8, A₂=0.577. UCL≈

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: If annual demand quadruples, the EOQ (other things equal) changes by a factor of:
questions.solution: EOQ ∝ √D, so a 4× demand gives √4 = 2× the order quantity.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Proportional hazards model (Cox):
options: h(t,Z) = h₀(t) · exp(b₁Z₁ + ... + bₙZₙ). Covariates: load, temperature, operator. Semi-parametric
questions.solution: Cox: baseline hazard h₀(t) arbitrary. β coefficients estimated by partial likelihood. HR = exp(β).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Anthropometry: design for 5th percentile female:
options: Ensures 95% of female population can use (reaches, fits)
questions.solution: Design: reach → 5th percentile female. Strength → 5th percentile male. Clearance → 95th percentile.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: An assembly line has a cycle time of 2 min and runs for 400 min/shift. The shift output is:
questions.solution: Output = available time / cycle time = 400/2 = 200 units.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Break-even: FC=$15000, SP=$45, VC=$15. BEP=

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Dodge-Romig sampling tables:
options: LTPD-based (single, double) and AOQL-based. Minimize inspection. For rectifying inspection
questions.solution: Dodge-Romig: process average p̄ affects plan. Manufacturer's risk considered. AOQL = 1%, 3%, 5% tables.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Process capability: Cpk = min[(USL-μ)/(3σ), (μ-LSL)/(3σ)]. For μ=10, σ=0.5, USL=12, LSL=8:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Total Quality Management (TQM) emphasizes:
options: Quality as the QC department's sole job
questions.solution: TQM is an organization-wide, customer-focused, continuous-improvement culture.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Series reliability: R₁=0.8, R₂=0.9. R_series=

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: With α = 0.2, last actual = 250 and last forecast = 280, the next exponential-smoothing forecast is:

questions.subject: Industrial Engineering
questions.stem: Sensitivity analysis in LP examines:
options: Effect of changing parameters on optimal solution
questions.solution: Shadow price = rate of change of Z with RHS.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Artificial variable cost in Big-M method:
options: Large penalty M to drive artificial to zero
questions.solution: Big-M: artificial variables have cost M (for minimization) or -M (for maximization). Drives to zero.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Periodic review (P-system):
options: Order up to target level every P periods. Larger safety stock than Q-system
questions.solution: P-system: order quantities vary. Count at review time. No perpetual inventory count needed.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Production Flow Analysis (PFA):
options: Machine-component matrix. Rearrange into cells. Rank order clustering (ROC). Bond energy algorithm
questions.solution: PFA: Burbidge. Use incidence matrix. ROC: binary rank. King's algorithm: sort rows/cols by binary value.

questions.subject: Industrial Engineering
questions.stem: Value Stream Mapping (VSM) identifies:
options: Value-added and non-value-added activities in a process
questions.solution: VSM: current state → future state. Map material and information flow. Identify waste. Prioritize improvements.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: With α = 0.5, last actual = 300 and last forecast = 180, the next exponential-smoothing forecast is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Make-to-order (MTO):
options: Produce after customer order. High customization, long lead time. No FG inventory
questions.solution: MTO: engineer-to-order (ETO) for complex. Assemble-to-order (ATO): modules pre-built.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Push vs pull systems:
questions.solution: Push: make-to-stock. Pull: make-to-order. Hybrid: push-pull boundary.
